triage.clinical_question: Does this high CGM glucose reading with a rising short-term trend require same-day review for possible treatment adjustment or contributing factors?
decision_factors: Current glucose is 238 mg/dL, above the supplied high threshold of 220 mg/dL.
Glucose has risen over the last hour (176 -> 214 -> 238 mg/dL), supporting a true upward trend.
Device signal quality is good and sensor age is reasonable, so artifact is less likely.
Baseline glucose is 142 mg/dL, indicating this is above the patient's usual level.

retrieval_query.clinical_question: Assess recent diabetes control and contributors to a sustained CGM hyperglycemia alert with upward trend.
ehr_topics: Recent insulin or antihyperglycemic medication changes
Last HbA1c and prior CGM/glucose trends
Recent acute illness, steroid use, or missed doses
anamnesis_topics: Symptoms of hyperglycemia such as polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, nausea
Recent food intake, missed medications, or pump/CGM issues
Any intercurrent illness, dehydration, or stress

relevant_notes.title: Diabetes medication adjustment
relevant_notes.text: Semaglutide initiated. Short-term glucose variability discussed during titration.

contradictions: No CGM timestamp, absolute value, or context for the reported high CGM reading is provided in the retrieved chunks, so same-day urgency cannot be confirmed from the data alone.
No explicit hypoglycemia, symptoms, ketones, or acute illness data are available.
missing_data: Exact CGM glucose value, timing, and duration of the rising trend.
Whether the patient has symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, dehydration, confusion) or ketone data.
Whether there were missed doses, dietary changes, steroid use, intercurrent illness, or other contributing factors.
Any clinician-specific threshold for same-day review or prior CGM targets.

symptoms_and_timeline: 2026-05-12: Feels well and reports no polyuria, vomiting, or confusion.
medication_adherence: 2026-05-12: Reports taking metformin and the new weekly injection as directed.
lifestyle_factors: 2026-05-12: Planned dietary change for a family celebration included a carbohydrate-heavy meal before the reading.
patient_concerns: Wants to know whether one high reading is dangerous.
missing_data: The actual CGM glucose value and exact trend details are not provided in the record.
No additional contributing factors, treatment changes, or follow-up plan are documented in the record.

brief.alert_summary: CGM alert shows glucose 238 mg/dL, above the supplied high threshold of 220 mg/dL, with a short-term rise over the prior hour. This is more consistent with a real hyperglycemic excursion than sensor artifact, but available data do not indicate an emergency. Same-day clinician review is reasonable for context and possible treatment adjustment.
brief.patient_snapshot: Patient has active type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity, with HbA1c 7.8% and fasting glucose 151 mg/dL on 2026-04-28. Semaglutide 0.5 mg weekly was started on 2026-05-05; the note mentions short-term glucose variability during titration. Patient reported feeling well on 2026-05-12, without polyuria, vomiting, or confusion, and reported taking metformin and the weekly injection as directed. A carbohydrate-heavy meal was planned before the reading.

contextual_analysis.text: The CGM alert value of 238 mg/dL exceeds the supplied high threshold of 220 mg/dL, and the trend 176 -> 214 -> 238 mg/dL supports a rising pattern.
Signal quality is reported as good and sensor age is 5 days, which lowers concern for device artifact.
Baseline glucose of 142 mg/dL is lower than the alert value, suggesting this is above the patient's usual level.
EHR data show suboptimal recent glycemic control with HbA1c 7.8% and fasting glucose 151 mg/dL, which supports a chronic tendency toward hyperglycemia.
Semaglutide was initiated recently, and the note explicitly mentions short-term glucose variability during titration.

Anamnesis indicates adherence to metformin and the new weekly injection, no polyuria/vomiting/confusion, and a carbohydrate-heavy meal before the reading, all of which may help contextualize the elevation.

risk_assessment.text: Persistent or repeated hyperglycemia may reflect incomplete current diabetes control or transient postprandial elevation, especially during recent medication titration.
The absence of reported symptoms such as vomiting, confusion, or polyuria reduces concern for immediate metabolic instability from the available history.
Because no ketone data, illness history, or duration of elevation are available, the clinical significance of this single alert cannot be fully determined from the record.

recommended_actions.action: Review the CGM event the same day with the patient or care team and compare it against recent glucose patterns and treatment timing.
recommended_actions.rationale: The reading is above threshold and rising, while the patient has known diabetes and recent medication titration.

recommended_actions.action: Confirm contributing factors: meal timing/composition, medication adherence, recent dose changes, and any intercurrent illness or steroid exposure.
recommended_actions.rationale: These factors could explain a transient rise and guide whether treatment adjustment is needed.

recommended_actions.action: Ask about hyperglycemia symptoms and whether any ketone testing is indicated per local diabetes protocol.
recommended_actions.rationale: Symptoms or ketones would increase concern for clinically important hyperglycemia.

recommended_actions.action: If readings remain elevated or recur, consider clinician-directed adjustment of the diabetes regimen after reviewing recent trends.
recommended_actions.rationale: Recent HbA1c and fasting glucose indicate room for improved control, but medication titration effects should be considered.

uncertainties_and_gaps: No exact duration of hyperglycemia beyond the short trend is provided.
No concurrent ketone data, acute illness history, or steroid exposure is available.
No clinician-specific threshold for same-day review or escalation is documented.
The patient-reported medication list includes metformin, but metformin is not present in the retrieved active EHR medication list.
The record does not show whether the reading was postprandial, pre-meal, or overnight.
brief.disclaimer: Educational prototype using simulated data. This output is not a diagnosis or medical advice and must be reviewed by a qualified clinician.
